title: The Happiest Baby on the Block

chapters.title: On being a parent
chapters.text:
Swaddling is the practice of snuggly wrapping babies in a cloth that restricts movement. It might seem counterintuitive to tightly wrap a fussy baby, as it can feel like you're forcing your struggling, sweating, and frustrated child to do something they don't want. However, it's important not to give up.

Snug wrapping is essential for relaxing days and evenings. Start by swaddling your infant the first ten times when they are quiet or sleepy, not when they’re uncomfortable and squirming. But what makes wrapping so effective? Swaddling offers multiple benefits that can significantly reduce restlessness in babies. Here are three ways swaddling reduces fussiness:

• Skin-to-skin cuddling is an ancient and time-tested calming method: When you wrap a baby in a soft cloth, they feel safe and warm as if they were cuddling with you.
• Swaddling replicates the womb experience: After birth, swaddling prevents babies from startling themselves with sudden movements, which can lead to increased agitation.
• Snug wrapping helps babies refocus away from discomfort: When babies cry, their heads flood with intense warning sounds. Each jerk triggers a new alert, making it hard for your baby to notice your soothing efforts.

Transitioning from the womb to the outside world is a big adjustment for babies.

Moreover, pay attention to your baby's lying position. Before birth, your baby never rested flat on their back. While back-sleeping is the only safe position for sleep, it's often the least comforting for crying newborns. Most babies enjoy lying on their backs when they're content but dislike it when they're irritable. Therefore, try to put them on their side in the fetal position when they are agitated. It activates position sensors in their muscles and inner ears, helping to maintain their calming reflex.

chapters.title: Caring without despairing
chapters.text:
Having a baby is a miraculous experience that brings joy, excitement, and a range of emotions. After giving birth, your priority will be caring for your baby and soothing their cries. Many parents feel proud and confident as they manage these tasks. However, it's common for some to feel discouraged when they struggle to understand their baby's needs.

I think America’s favorite pastime isn’t baseball.…It’s giving new moms unsolicited advice. ~ Harvey Karp, MD

The truth is that no one anticipates a challenging newborn, so it's natural to feel overwhelmed when your child's cries persist despite your efforts. Anxiety is also common among parents, especially when they are sleep-deprived. So here are some simple concepts that can explain how to calm a crying baby and improve sleep for everyone:

• The missing fourth trimester: Newborns are still developing and run, walk, or even burp without help. Mimicking the womb by carrying your baby as if you were still pregnant triggers a deep, neurological calming reflex in your baby's brain.
• The calming reflex: For generations, experienced caregivers have intuitively rocked and shushed newborns to soothe them. These actions replicate the repetitive sounds and motions of the womb, which babies find comforting.
• The 5 S’s: It stands for swaddling, side/stomach sleeping, shushing, swinging, and sucking.

A baby's cry isn't always a bad sign; it's a natural form of communication.

chapters.title: When crying becomes excessive
chapters.text:
During the first few months, most babies cry because they need to, not because they want to. They don't realize they're sending you a message. Instead, they cry to express their discomfort, and you end up overhearing these little "monologues." Your infant will soon realize that their crying gets your attention, and by four months, they'll develop a small language of coos, bleats, and yelps to indicate specific needs.

Be actively involved in your child's life; don't worry that responding to their cries will teach them undesirable behaviors. As your baby approaches nine months old, setting limits becomes crucial, but it's essential to do so without undermining the trust you've built with your child.

Ignoring your child doesn't foster independence; it only teaches them to feel alone.

Even a baby's piercing cries can simply be a sign of impatience. And once they get what they need, they usually quiet down. But if your baby doesn't stop crying, they might have colic — a condition where a healthy, well-fed baby cries for more than three hours a day, three days a week, for at least three weeks.

There are five possible triggers of colic:

• Minor digestive issues: gas, constipation, or discomfort
• Major digestive problems: food intolerance or allergy, an overabundance of harmful bacteria, or acid reflux
• Maternal anxiety: babies sensing their mother's stress
• Neurological immaturity: overstimulation in babies with underdeveloped nervous systems
• Challenging temperament: oversensitivity to mild disruptions in highly sensitive babies

The good news is that colic is treatable. If your baby shows these symptoms, consult a pediatrician for proper diagnosis and treatment.

chapters.title: What colic hides
chapters.text:
The root cause of colic has long been a source of discussion. After years of myths and misunderstandings, it's now clear that colic often occurs when babies are deprived of the womb's calming rhythms. Reassuring your child that you'll always be there for them with comforting arms and warm milk builds trust and lays the foundation for future relationships and connections.

There’s a reason behind everything in nature. ~ Aristotle

Babies with easy temperaments and good self-regulation can usually handle their exciting new environment. But what if your baby struggles with self-soothing or misses the rhythms of the womb? In such cases, too much fuss or too little movement can lead to colic.

Keeping your baby close in the first few months helps them feel secure.

Historically, our ancestors lived in close-knit communities and traveled less, spending more time carrying and gently moving their newborns. When babies lack these experiences, they often become fussier and cry more. Some parents misinterpreted this reaction as a sign that their delicate infants were overstimulated, leading them to use gentler rocking and softer sounds. While loving gestures are essential for keeping newborns quiet, jiggly movements and loud sounds are often necessary to soothe crying babies.

Here are ten ways to mimic the womb environment, helping your baby feel secure and calm:

• Holding
• Dancing
• Rocking
• Swinging
• Swaddling
• Feeding
• White noise
• Singing
• Pacifiers
• Smart sleepers

chapters.title: Swaddling: comfort or confinement?
chapters.text:
Swaddling is the practice of snuggly wrapping babies in a cloth that restricts movement. It might seem counterintuitive to tightly wrap a fussy baby, as it can feel like you're forcing your struggling, sweating, and frustrated child to do something they don't want. However, it's important not to give up.

Snug wrapping is essential for relaxing days and evenings. Start by swaddling your infant the first ten times when they are quiet or sleepy, not when they’re uncomfortable and squirming. But what makes wrapping so effective? Swaddling offers multiple benefits that can significantly reduce restlessness in babies. Here are three ways swaddling reduces fussiness:

• Skin-to-skin cuddling is an ancient and time-tested calming method: When you wrap a baby in a soft cloth, they feel safe and warm as if they were cuddling with you.
• Swaddling replicates the womb experience: After birth, swaddling prevents babies from startling themselves with sudden movements, which can lead to increased agitation.
• Snug wrapping helps babies refocus away from discomfort: When babies cry, their heads flood with intense warning sounds. Each jerk triggers a new alert, making it hard for your baby to notice your soothing efforts.

Transitioning from the womb to the outside world is a big adjustment for babies.

Moreover, pay attention to your baby's lying position. Before birth, your baby never rested flat on their back. While back-sleeping is the only safe position for sleep, it's often the least comforting for crying newborns. Most babies enjoy lying on their backs when they're content but dislike it when they're irritable. Therefore, try to put them on their side in the fetal position when they are agitated. It activates position sensors in their muscles and inner ears, helping to maintain their calming reflex.

chapters.title: “I like to move it” in the babies’ world
chapters.text:
In the early 1970s, researchers discovered that the womb is surprisingly noisy. The sound of blood flowing through arteries creates a rumbling noise measuring 75 to 92 decibels — louder than a vacuum cleaner! Some parents worry this volume might overwhelm their babies and cause more fussiness.

Interestingly, fetuses don't perceive these sounds as loudly as you might think. They're surrounded by water, have waxy vernix in their ear canals, fluid in their middle ears, and thick, rigid eardrums, reducing their hearing volume. Imagine your baby's surprise when they enter your peaceful world of whispers and silence after being in the noisy womb. This stark contrast can be unsettling for newborns.

Similarly, parents often worry about swinging or moving rhythmically with a baby. But newborns actually love it, and gentle bouncing gives them a sense of security. Swinging mimics the familiar motions they experienced in the womb and triggers their calming reflex.

All techniques are suitable for soothing a baby if they are safe.

However, all movement must be gentle and controlled. Never shake your baby when you’re angry or frustrated. Sadly, about 1,000 babies, averaging three months old, are victims of Shaken Baby Syndrome (SBS) each year, a form of traumatic head trauma. Only about 25% of these victims survive, often with severe brain damage. In short, rhythmic movement is highly recommended to calm babies, but shaking is extremely dangerous and should never be done.

Did you know? Research shows that typical infant fussiness usually starts at one to three weeks, peaks around six to eight weeks, and generally subsides by three to four months.

chapters.title: What’s so good about pacifiers?
chapters.text:
Many people believe that some parents have a special gift for calming babies. Inexperienced parents might watch in awe as older relatives skillfully swaddle or put their newborn to sleep. Nonetheless, this mastery comes from both experience and knowledge. Learning and applying the 5 S's can dramatically improve your parenting skills.

Sucking is a crucial calming technique because it's a natural self-soothing method babies use even before birth. However, few babies continue this practice after leaving the womb. It's not that they don't want to — they simply can't. Sticking a finger in their mouth and keeping it there is challenging for newborns. Even when they try, their weak muscles make it more likely they'll smack their nose than find their mouth. That is why offering a breast, bottle, or pacifier calms them immediately.

Observation and practice turn good parenting into great parenting.

Fortunately, in addition to breastfeeding, we have pacifiers for babies whose primary ‘calmness language’ is sucking. Here are some tips for enhancing those abilities:

• Pick the right nipple: The best pacifier for your baby is the one they prefer. Take your time to study which one your baby likes most.
• Don't force it: Trying to put a pacifier in a crying baby's mouth usually fails. Calm them first, then offer the pacifier once they've settled down.
• Use gentle resistance: When your infant is calm, give them a pacifier. Once they start sucking, gently tug it as if you're about to take it out. After a few repetitions, your baby will likely suck harder to keep it in place.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Having a baby can be challenging, especially when dealing with stress and postpartum recovery. While some babies are naturally calm, others need extra attention. Therefore, the more demanding your newborn is, the more care they'll need.

Parenting demands patience and commitment, particularly when soothing your child. By focusing on understanding your baby's needs, you'll find it easier to identify the cause of their discomfort. Thus, the calmer your baby becomes, the more exciting and pleasant your parenting experience will be.

Try this

• Aim for frequent skin-to-skin contact with your baby.
• Invest time in learning about parenting techniques and child development.
• Keep your partner or family informed, and don't hesitate to ask for help when you feel exhausted or sleep-deprived.
• Watch “Harvey Karp’s secrets to calming a fussy baby” on YouTube to learn more about the nuances of the 5 S's technique.

Read it till the end?
